race.name: Wish One Millau
race.tagline: France's UCI Gravel World Series round — the Grands Causses above the Millau Viaduct.

vitals.date_specific: 2027: June 13 (SUNDAY pattern; 2026 ran Sun Jun 14 — Drechou/Wright won)
terrain_types: UCI Gravel World Series (sole French round)
vitals.registration: Online. Organizer: Wish One (first-ever UCI gravel event on European soil, 2022)

terrain.surface: Gravel roads, forest track, and paved sections through the Grands Causses limestone plateau (~40% forest, 40% gravel, 20% paved). Opens with the Puech d'Agast climb (+450m in the first 8 kilometres) straight out of Millau.
features: Puech d'Agast opening climb (+450m in the first 8km, cold-open)
Grands Causses limestone plateau (~40% forest / 40% gravel / 20% paved)
Millau Viaduct finish views (world's tallest bridge)
UCI Gravel World Series — sole French round; official 80km course for women 50+/men 60+

gravel_god_rating.score_note: Regional French event. Plateau terrain with moderate organization and regional field.

biased_opinion.verdict: UCI Gravel World Series — France's Founding Round
biased_opinion.summary: Wish One Millau is France's sole round of the UCI Gravel World Series and the first-ever UCI gravel event held on European soil (2022). The course opens with the Puech d'Agast climb (450m in the first 8km) straight out of Millau, then spends 130km and 2,500m of climbing across the Grands Causses limestone plateaus before finishing beneath the Millau Viaduct. ~600 riders from 20 nations race it; women 50+ and men 60+ race an official 80km UCI course.
strengths: UCI Gravel World Series pedigree — Worlds qualification on the line
Grands Causses plateau scenery and the Millau Viaduct finish
Cold-open Puech d'Agast climb makes an honest, immediate selection
Official age-group course (women 50+/men 60+) — real masters racing
weaknesses: Rural Aveyron access — nearest airports are 1.5-2 hours out
Regional-scale organization compared to the US majors
June timing hits peak tourism season, raising lodging costs
Limited course variety beyond the two headline distances
biased_opinion.bottom_line: A genuinely significant race wrapped in a regional-scale event — the UCI World Series status and the Puech d'Agast opener make it worth targeting for climbers, especially anyone in France or Europe. Not a bucket-list travel race for most Americans, but not just a local fondo either.

final_verdict.one_liner: French plateau gravel in Millau Grands Causses.
final_verdict.should_you_race: If you're in France/Europe and want a local event—yes, it's solid. If you're international—there are better options.
final_verdict.alternatives: For better French events: 66° South Pyrénées. For similar regional events: other French gravel races.

logistics.food: Millau has full dining options. Stock nutrition.

history.origin_story: Launched in 2022 as the first-ever UCI gravel event held on European soil. It remains the sole French round of the UCI Gravel World Series.
notable_moments: First-ever UCI gravel race on European soil (2022)
Toby Perry's solo winning attack from 59.5km to go, finishing in 4:34:43 (2023)
2026: Hugo Drechou (men) and Sophie Wright (women) won
history.reputation: Sole French round of the UCI Gravel World Series; ~600 riders from 20 nations.

course_description.character: Wish One's Sunday UCI GWS round from Millau (~1,608ft): UCI 130km/2,500m (81mi/8,200ft) flagship for elite/open ages; UCI 80km/1,500m = official course for women 50+/men 60+; plus non-qualifying Open 40/80km waves (U17/U19). Opens with the Puech d'Agast climb (+450m in the first 8km) onto the Larzac/Grands Causses plateaus — ~40% forest, 40% gravel track, 20% paved — finishing with views of the Millau Viaduct (world's tallest cable-stayed bridge). ~600 riders, 20+ nations. First-ever UCI gravel event on European soil (2022). RACE DAY IS SUNDAY. PRIOR PROFILE 70mi/4,500ft matched nothing — corrected 2026-07-14.

suffering_zones.label: Puech d'Agast
suffering_zones.desc: The course's opening climb — 450 metres of ascent in the first 8 kilometres, straight out of Millau on cold legs.

suffering_zones.label: Grands Causses Plateau
suffering_zones.desc: Sustained limestone plateau climbing across the Larzac — forest track and gravel, grades that keep returning.

suffering_zones.label: Final Approach
suffering_zones.desc: The closing kilometres toward Millau, finishing with the Millau Viaduct in view.

guide_variables.race_terrain: Grands Causses limestone plateau — forest track, gravel, and paved roads
race_challenges: Puech d'Agast cold-open climb (450m in the first 8km)

non_negotiables.why: The course's hardest sustained effort starts at kilometre zero — 450m of climbing in the first 8km, on cold legs.
2,500m of climbing spreads across the Grands Causses plateaus, not one hard hill — repeatable strength matters more than a single effort.
Mid-June in the Aveyron runs warm and bright for a 5+ hour day.

marketplace_variables.race_hook: France's UCI Gravel World Series round — the Grands Causses above the Millau Viaduct.
marketplace_variables.race_hook_detail: First-ever UCI gravel event on European soil (2022); sole French round of the UCI Gravel World Series.

logistics.explanation: Rural Millau requires connections through Rodez (75km), Montpellier (122km), or Toulouse (208km) airports with rental car necessity. Hugo Drechou praised "support from the fans" suggesting local engagement, but no packet pickup or lodging guidance provided in race materials.

length.explanation: Elite pacing patterns show the 130km distance creates tactical racing opportunities. Toby Perry launched his winning attack at 59.5km to go in 2023, finishing solo in 4:34:43, while 2024 saw sprint finishes with top contenders matching times at 4:25:26. The distance allows for sustained efforts without soul-crushing single climbs. Similar distance to Crusher in the Tushar.

technicality.explanation: Course features 50/50 split between gravel and road surfaces and is "designed to be fast" according to organizers. The route includes "technical sections throughout the course" with "little time to rest," though the overall emphasis on speed suggests moderate rather than extreme technical demands through the Causses terrain. Less technical than The Mid South.

elevation.explanation: The 2,500m elevation gain creates consistent selection pressure through rolling terrain. Race reports note "another hard climb awaits between kilometers 30 and 40" following the opening climb out of Millau valley, with the finish featuring a "paved 2km climb" under the viaduct that determines final positions in close races. Similar elevation profile to Gravel Worlds.

climate.explanation: Puncture rates suggest challenging surface conditions, with Rosa Kloser suffering double punctures at km 95 in 2023 and Noah Adhemard forced to DNF from similar mechanical issues. The Causses limestone terrain appears demanding on equipment, though no severe weather disruptions reported across recent editions. Similar climate challenges to SBT GRVL.

altitude.explanation: Starting in Millau valley with course majority across Causses plateaus keeps altitude impacts minimal. The terrain rolls between moderate elevations without reaching performance-affecting heights, allowing riders from sea level backgrounds to compete without acclimatization concerns across the limestone landscape features. Modest altitude compared to Belgian Waffle Ride San Diego.

adventure.explanation: The exact route stays "under wraps until twenty-four hours before the start due to it passing through a military zone," adding mystery to the Larzac military camp access. Racing through restricted terrain with "exceptional tracks" unavailable elsewhere delivers unique adventure value through protected Grands Causses landscapes.

prestige.explanation: UCI Gravel World Series status draws elite talent including Andréa Vendrame, who finished 18th overall and qualified for Gravel World Championships. Julian Lino notes the UCI series helps gravel "create access for everybody" as the discipline grows beyond its US roots, though the event remains regionally focused.

race_quality.explanation: Course design emphasizes speed with tactical opportunities, creating varied race outcomes from solo breakaways to sprint finishes. However, the demanding concentration requirements where "zoning out is down right impossible" during "five plus hours in the saddle" suggests organizational challenges in managing rider safety across technical terrain.

experience.explanation: Racing finishes under the Millau Viaduct, "the tallest bridge in the world with its height of 336.4 meters," creating spectacular theater. Russell Finsterwald "ventured to France to get a feel for European gravel racing," highlighting the cultural immersion value beyond just the competitive experience.

community.explanation: 600 riders from 20 countries participated in 2023, with the 2025 single-day format "offering a more friendly and intense atmosphere between the riders." Celebrity participation includes F1 champion Alain Prost, while organizers emphasize their mission to "inspire people to get out there and ride" beyond pure competition.

field_depth.explanation: Elite competitions show limited depth with large finishing gaps typical. Carolin Schiff won by 12+ minutes in 2023, while race reports note "significant gaps between the following riders" as standard. The field attracts quality leaders but lacks the depth for close racing throughout categories. Less competitive depth than Almanzo 100.

value.explanation: UCI World Championships qualification through top 25% placement adds tangible competitive value for serious racers. The single-day 2025 format with multiple distance options (40km to 130km) accommodates different experience levels, while military camp access provides unique terrain unavailable elsewhere in European gravel racing.

expenses.explanation: Registration runs €60-100. International access requires flights to distant airports plus rental car necessity for rural Aveyron travel. June timing hits peak tourism season in the Millau region, increasing accommodation costs without race-specific lodging partnerships or shuttle services to reduce the logistical expense burden for visiting riders. Higher total cost than Badlands.

primary.name: Specialized Pathfinder Pro
primary.why: Fast center, grippy edges. The modern all-rounder benchmark.

primary.name: Panaracer GravelKing SK
primary.why: Affordable all-rounder. Light knobs add grip without killing speed.

primary.name: Vittoria Terreno Mix
primary.why: Graphene versatility. Mixed-terrain grip with reasonable rolling speed.

front_rear_split.rationale: Faster front for reduced rolling resistance on straights, grippier rear for traction on loose climbs and corners

Découvrez les premières images de la 3ème édition de la Wish One Millau Grands Causses qui a vu la victoire chez les Elite Femmes de Carolin Schiff et de Florian Dauphin chez les Elite Hommes.

videos.title: Wish One Millau Grands Causses 2023
videos.description:
Revivez l'édition 2023 de la Wish One Millau Grands Causses (manche française des Trek Gravel World Series).
146km et 2500m de D+ * qui ont vu la victoire de Carolin Schiff et Toby Perry  sur les routes et chemins de Terre de Gravel du Parc Naturel Régional des Grands Causses !

*100km et 1700m de D+ pour les femmes de +50ans et les hommes de +60ans
 @TotalEnergies @trekbikes @ucichannel @ParcGrandsCausses @RégionOccitanie @TourismeAveyron @TotalEnergies_procycling 
#gravelrace #millau #gravel #gravelbike
videos.curation_reason: Comprehensive race recap from the official organizer showcasing the challenging 146km/2500m+ course through Parc Naturel Régional des Grands Causses.

videos.title: Je roule en Coupe du Monde Gravel UCI à Millau... et je n'ai pas le niveau...😜

rider_intel.search_text: Based on available race footage from the Wish One Millau gravel race in France's Millau Grands Causses region, riders specifically mention heat as a notable factor during the 70-mile event. The race covers 4500 feet of elevation gain through the French countryside. While detailed course information is limited in available transcripts, the emphasis on heat conditions suggests riders should prepare for warm weather racing in this region of France. The race appears to be part of organized gravel racing events in the area, though specific terrain details, technical sections, and gear recommendations are not detailed in the current race footage transcripts.
